summary: This cluster of research focuses on the metabolic versatility of microalgae, specifically their ability to operate under autotrophic, heterotrophic, and mixotrophic growth regimes to remediate carbon-rich waste streams. A central theme is the targeted removal of nutrient-derived pollutants, particularly nitrates and dissolved organic nitrogen, from wastewater through enhanced bioremediation strategies. The studies emphasize the manipulation of metabolic pathways and the identification of extremophilic algal strains to improve the efficiency of this nutrient removal process. The geographic and ecological scope is broadly applicable to wastewater treatment systems, with a shared goal of leveraging microalgae for environmental remediation.
